name: Simon de Montfort
fullName: Simon de Montfort, 6th Earl of Leicester
role: 6th Earl of Leicester; leader of the baronial reform movement

titles.title: De facto governor of king and kingdom (head of the baronial council after Lewes)

citations.title: Montfort, Simon of (1208?-1265) (DNB00)
citations.quote: From that moment Simon was virtually governor of king and kingdom.

summary: Simon de Montfort, 6th Earl of Leicester, was an English nobleman of French birth who became the leading voice of baronial opposition to Henry III. A central figure behind the Provisions of Oxford of 1258, he led the baronial cause into open war and, after his victory at the Battle of Lewes in May 1264, held effective control of royal government. In January 1265 he summoned a parliament that for the first time drew elected representatives from the towns as well as the shires, a step later celebrated as a milestone in the growth of parliamentary government. He was defeated and killed at the Battle of Evesham on 4 August 1265.

citations.title: Simon de Montfort, 6th Earl of Leicester
citations.quote: who led the baronial opposition to the rule of King Henry III, culminating in the Second Barons' War

citations.title: Simon de Montfort, 6th Earl of Leicester
citations.quote: Montfort sent his summons, in the king's name, to each county and to a select list of boroughs, asking each to send two representatives.

citations.title: Montfort, Simon of (1208?-1265) (DNB00)
citations.quote: At the close of a three hours massacre...he fell, almost the last of his little band, crying 'God's grace!' as he passed away.

uncertain: His birth year is not firmly attested; the DNB states only that he was 'probably born about 1208.'

summary: Ramon Berenguer V, of the House of Barcelona, ruled as Count of Provence and Forcalquier through the first half of the thirteenth century. He married Beatrice of Savoy in 1219, and the couple's four daughters all became queens: Margaret married Louis IX of France, Eleanor married Henry III of England, Sanchia married Richard, Earl of Cornwall (later King of the Romans), and Beatrice married Charles I of Sicily. He died at Aix-en-Provence in August 1245.

citations.title: Ramon Berenguer V, Count of Provence
citations.quote: Eleanor of Provence (1223-1291): married Henry III, King of England

uncertain: The Wikipedia article on his wife, Beatrice of Savoy, gives their marriage as December 1220 rather than 5 June 1219 as stated on his own page; the earlier date, sourced to his own article, is used here.

summary: Beatrice of Savoy, daughter of Thomas I of Savoy and Margaret of Geneva, married Ramon Berenguer V, Count of Provence. Their four daughters, Margaret, Eleanor, Sanchia, and Beatrice, all married reigning kings, giving Beatrice a central place in the dynastic politics of thirteenth-century Europe, including as mother-in-law of Henry III of England through her daughter Eleanor of Provence.

uncertain: Her own Wikipedia article dates her marriage to December 1220, while her husband's article gives 5 June 1219; the latter is used here as the date attested on his page.

summary: Sanchia of Provence, third daughter of Ramon Berenguer V, Count of Provence, and Beatrice of Savoy, and younger sister of Eleanor of Provence, married Richard, Earl of Cornwall, at Westminster in November 1243. When Richard was elected King of the Romans in 1257 she was crowned alongside him at Aachen. She died at Berkhamsted Castle in 1261.

summary: Frederick II, of the House of Hohenstaufen, was Holy Roman Emperor, King of Sicily, and, through his first marriage, King of Jerusalem, ruling as one of the most powerful sovereigns of thirteenth-century Europe. In July 1235 he married as his third wife Isabella of England, sister of Henry III, who was crowned with him at Worms. He died at Castel Fiorentino in Sicily in December 1250.

summary: Alexander III became King of Scots in 1249 at the age of seven and married Margaret of England, daughter of Henry III, at York Minster on Christmas Day 1251. His reign saw the securing of the Western Isles from Norway after the Battle of Largs. He died in March 1286 after a fall from his horse while riding at night, a death that precipitated the succession crisis leading to the Wars of Scottish Independence.

summary: John Hastings, 2nd Earl of Pembroke, son of Laurence Hastings, 1st Earl of Pembroke, was married as a child to Margaret of England, a daughter of Edward III, in May 1359; she died shortly afterwards without the marriage having been consummated. He later married Anne Mauny in 1368 and succeeded to the earldom of Pembroke that year, dying in Picardy in 1375.

summary: David II, son of Robert the Bruce, became King of Scots on his father's death in 1329 at the age of five. He was married as a child to Joan of the Tower, daughter of Edward II of England, under the terms of the Anglo-Scottish peace settlement. His reign was marked by long exile in France, capture by the English at the Battle of Neville's Cross, and eleven years of captivity. He died at Edinburgh Castle in 1371, the last of the House of Bruce to rule Scotland.

citations.title: David II of Scotland
citations.quote: David was not buried...beside his parents at Dunfermline Abbey, but before the high altar of Holyrood Abbey.

summary: Ralph de Monthermer, a squire of unknown parentage in the service of Gilbert de Clare, secretly married the widowed Joan of Acre, daughter of Edward I, in January 1297, briefly provoking the king's anger before being recognised as Earl of Gloucester and Hertford in his wife's right. He lost the earldoms on Joan's death in 1307 but continued to be summoned to parliament as Lord Monthermer.

citations.quote: Of unknown parentage, Monthermer was born in Wales.

uncertain: His second marriage, around 1313 to Isabel le Despenser, widow of Lord Hastings, is not recorded here as a spouse entry because she is not otherwise part of this dataset.

summary: Margaret Wake, daughter of John Wake, 1st Baron Wake of Liddell, married Edmund of Woodstock, Earl of Kent and half-brother of Edward II, in late 1325, and was left a widow with young children when Edmund was executed as a traitor in 1330. Their daughter Joan of Kent later married Edward the Black Prince. Margaret briefly held the barony of Wake of Liddell in her own right in 1349, the year she died of plague.

summary: Enguerrand VII, Lord of Coucy, one of the great nobles of France, married Isabella of England, eldest daughter of Edward III, at Windsor in 1365 and was made Earl of Bedford in England the following year. He remained active in French politics and warfare, and was captured at the Battle of Nicopolis in 1396 during a crusade against the Ottomans; weakened by his wounds, he died in captivity at Bursa of bubonic plague in February 1397.

citations.title: Enguerrand VII de Coucy
citations.quote: Captured at the Battle of Nicopolis in 1396

uncertain: As with Isabella of England's own record, the end of the marriage is left unresolved: Isabella's date of death is genuinely disputed between sources (1379 or 1382), predating Enguerrand's own death in 1397, and no source consulted here states when his English earldom of Bedford lapsed.

name: Thomas Holland
role: 1st Earl of Kent jure uxoris (1st husband of Joan of Kent)

citations.title: Thomas Holland, 1st Earl of Kent
citations.quote: 28 December 1360 (aged around 46)

citations.title: Joan of Kent
citations.quote: In 1340, at the age of about twelve, Joan secretly married 26-year-old Thomas Holland

summary: Thomas Holland, a soldier who fought at the Battle of Crecy and the siege of Caen and was a founding Knight of the Order of the Garter in 1348, secretly married Joan of Kent, granddaughter of Edward I, in 1340 while both were young; the marriage was contested for years by a rival match forced on Joan with William Montagu, and only settled in Holland's favour by papal ruling in 1349. Through Joan he was recognised as Earl of Kent jure uxoris shortly before his death on campaign in Normandy in December 1360.

citations.title: Thomas Holland, 1st Earl of Kent
citations.quote: In 1348, he was invested as one of the founders and 13th Knight of the new Order of the Garter

uncertain: Joan of Kent's marital history is genuinely contested in the sources: she secretly married Holland in 1340, was separately married to William Montagu while Holland was overseas, and the dispute between the two unions went to the papal curia, which ruled for Holland's marriage as valid in 1349. This record does not attempt to resolve the underlying contemporary confusion beyond reporting the papal ruling.

name: William Montagu
role: 2nd Earl of Salisbury (contested husband of Joan of Kent)

citations.title: William Montagu, 2nd Earl of Salisbury
citations.quote: 25 June 1328 in Donyatt in Somerset, England

citations.title: William Montagu, 2nd Earl of Salisbury
citations.quote: 3 June 1397 (aged 68)

citations.title: Joan of Kent
citations.quote: In 1349, the proceedings ruled in Holland's favour. Pope Clement VI annulled Joan's marriage to Salisbury.

citations.title: William Montagu, 2nd Earl of Salisbury
citations.quote: Earl of Salisbury and Baron Montagu from 1344-1397

summary: William Montagu, 2nd Earl of Salisbury, married Joan of Kent while both were teenagers in the early 1340s, unaware, on his side, that she had already secretly married Thomas Holland. When Holland returned from campaign in 1348 and pressed his claim, an ecclesiastical inquiry found in Holland's favour, and the Pope annulled Joan's marriage to Montagu in 1349. Montagu later married Elizabeth Mohun and lived at Bisham Manor in Berkshire; he died in 1397.

citations.title: William Montagu, 2nd Earl of Salisbury
citations.quote: Following the findings of the inquiry, Lord Salisbury's marriage with Joan, Fair Maid of Kent, was annulled by the Pope in 1349.

uncertain: Joan of Kent's marital status between 1340 and 1349 is a genuinely contested case that went to the Pope; contemporaries and later historians differ on whether Montagu's marriage was ever valid or merely a family-arranged match made while Holland was believed permanently absent. This record reports the papal ruling without treating the underlying contemporary dispute as settled. His second marriage, to Elizabeth Mohun, is not recorded as a spouse entry because she is not otherwise part of this dataset.
